# FDA’s Role in Veterinary Regenerative Medicine

[Español](/animal-veterinary/cell-and-tissue-products-animals/el-papel-de-la-fda-en-la-medicina-regenerativa-veterinaria "El papel de la FDA en la medicina regenerativa veterinaria")

In the fast-growing field of veterinary regenerative medicine, material from cells and tissues — such as living cells, serum, or bone — is used in animals with the hope of repairing diseased or damaged tissues or organs. For example, in a horse with tendonitis, the damaged tendon may heal with scar tissue that isn’t as strong or elastic as the original tendon before it was damaged. The goal is to transplant living cells or other tissues into the injured tendon, hopefully stimulating it to regenerate and heal.

Veterinary regenerative medicine is an active area of research for developing new cell and tissue therapies for animals. Such therapies are called animal cells, tissues, and cell- and tissue-based products, or ACTPs for short. This term refers to products for use in animals that contain, consist of, or are derived from cells or tissues. Examples include animal stem cells, differentiated cells, and tissues such as blood, platelet-rich plasma, and amnion.

ACTPs are intended to be implanted, transplanted, infused, or transferred from a donor into an animal recipient. Sometimes the donor and recipient are the same animal. Sometimes the donor and recipient are different animals of the same species. Other times, the donor and recipient are different species.

## Most ACTPs are Regulated by FDA

Most ACTPs are regulated by FDA under the Federal Food, Drug, and Cosmetic Act because they are intended to treat, control, or prevent a disease or other condition — such as osteoarthritis — or to affect the structure or function of the body — such as improving fertility. FDA must review and approve these products before companies can legally market them.

The pre-market review is integral to FDA’s ability to protect animal and public health. During the review, the agency evaluates information submitted by the company to make sure the product is safe and effective for its intended use or uses and that the product is properly manufactured and adequately labeled and packaged. If FDA determines that the data show the product is safe, effective, and high-quality, the agency approves the product for the use or uses listed on the label.

## Navigating the Approval Process

FDA is committed to helping those involved in the manufacturing of ACTPs navigate the approval process. The agency understands the need to have safe and effective ACTPs approved and available for use in animals and has taken several actions to encourage the development of these products.

FDA has issued several guidance documents to help developers of ACTPs better understand the regulatory process and associated approval requirements; conducted outreach by presenting at public scientific conferences and publishing web resources about the regulatory process for ACTPs, such as the [Q&As for Developers of ACTPs](/animal-veterinary/cell-and-tissue-products-animals/qa-developers-animal-cells-tissues-and-cell-and-tissue-based-products-actps "Q&A for Developers of Animal Cells, Tissues, and Cell- and Tissue-Based Products (ACTPs)"); provided letters to [universities](/animal-veterinary/product-safety-information/fda-letter-universities-development-animal-cells-tissues-and-cell-and-tissue-based-products-actps "FDA letter to universities on the development of animal cells, tissues, and cell- and tissue-based products (ACTPs)") and [veterinary schools](/animal-veterinary/product-safety-information/fdas-letter-veterinary-schools-regarding-animal-cell-based-products "FDA’s Letter to Veterinary Schools regarding Animal Cell-Based Products") on the development of ACTPs; and started the [Veterinary Innovation Program](/animal-veterinary/biotechnology-products-cvm-animals-and-animal-food/vip-veterinary-innovation-program "VIP: Veterinary Innovation Program") (VIP) and its expansion, [VIP Plus](/animal-veterinary/biotechnology-products-cvm-animals-and-animal-food/vip-plus "VIP Plus"), for developers of animal biotechnology products, including ACTPs. For more information on the review process, please see [Cell and Tissue Products for Animals](/animal-veterinary/biotechnology-products-cvm-animals-and-animal-food/cell-and-tissue-products-animals "Cell and Tissue Products for Animals").

## Unapproved ACTPs

An ACTP that is regulated by FDA but marketed without approval is an unapproved ACTP. It is illegal to market an unapproved ACTP because it hasn’t gone through the required FDA pre-market review and approval process. Unapproved ACTPs may not be safe, effective, properly manufactured, or adequately labeled or packaged. Currently, no ACTPs are FDA-approved.

FDA recognizes that certain ACTPs, while unapproved, pose a lower risk to human and animal health than other categories of ACTPs. The agency intends to prioritize its review of certain ACTPs in a way that protects human and animal health by making decisions based on risk and informed by available science and data. FDA provides a [publicly available list](/animal-veterinary/cell-and-tissue-products-animals/animal-cells-tissues-and-cell-and-tissue-based-products-actps-risk-reviewed-actps "Animal Cells, Tissues, and Cell- and Tissue-Based Products (ACTPs): Risk-Reviewed ACTPs") of those ACTPs that have undergone a risk-based review of product-specific information, and the agency does not expect the developer to submit an application for approval before marketing it. FDA has conducted a risk-based review and determined that the agency understands the risks and that there are no safety concerns for which the agency would need additional data to address. If FDA becomes aware of new safety information, the agency may revisit this decision.

## Clinical Field Studies

FDA keeps a list of [current clinical field studies that are investigating the use of ACTPs](/animal-veterinary/cell-and-tissue-products-animals/clinical-field-studies-animal-cells-tissues-and-cell-and-tissue-based-products-actps "Clinical Field Studies for Animal Cells, Tissues, and Cell- and Tissue-Based Products (ACTPs)") in animals. (A clinical field study is a type of research study that tests how well a new medical product works in animals.) The companies conducting these studies have given FDA permission to include their studies in the publicly available list, which includes information about the condition being studied and in which species. Depending on the study, enrolled animals may be pets, such as dogs and cats, or food-producing animals, such as cattle and pigs. There is also contact information for each study, so the public can find out further information.

## Great Promise but Proof Needed

In recent years, the scientific community has made significant advances in developing ACTPs, and scientists continue to research these innovative therapies. Veterinary regenerative medicine holds great promise for future safe and effective treatments for many animal diseases and other conditions. However, the ultimate proof rests in obtaining high quality data from well-conducted, well-controlled, and well-designed scientific studies. The results of these studies will help FDA, veterinarians, animal owners, and developers of ACTPs better understand the potential benefits and risks of these products.

Because of the novelty and complexity of ACTPs, FDA encourages individuals, universities, and companies interested in developing these products to contact the agency. For questions about an animal biotechnology process or product, please contact the agency at [AskCVM-Biotech@fda.hhs.gov](mailto:AskCVM-Biotech@fda.hhs.gov). For information on other product types or for all other general inquiries, contact [AskCVM@fda.hhs.gov](mailto:AskCVM@fda.hhs.gov).

# Regenerative medicine

[Comment on this policy](https://ebusiness.avma.org/misc/policycommenting.aspx?pid=C5C63AD26F564E0B917851B0D66F571D)

Regenerative medicine includes the use of biological therapies to regenerate or replace cells that are injured, diseased, or defective to restore normal function. These therapies include orthobiologic therapies, stem cell, cell-based, and gene editing therapies. Some examples include serum or plasma products and stem cell products to effect therapeutic benefit in disease states. While regenerative medicine holds promise of improvements in the treatment of a variety of diseases, many of which lack adequately effective treatments, questions remain. The AVMA supports the continued scientific development of these modalities while at the same time encouraging its members to employ caution with respect to their use.

While data continue to accumulate suggesting therapeutic benefit from regenerative medicine, published peer-reviewed studies definitively documenting benefit or safety are still lacking for many diseases. Nor has a scientific consensus for effective treatment regimen including indication, route of administration, dosing frequency, duration or technique been developed. Despite these scientific insufficiencies, the adoption of regenerative medicine in the veterinary profession has grown rapidly. Some therapies being propounded and the processes and equipment being sold have outpaced the science that supports them. Veterinarians have few guidelines and limited resources for differentiating valid and effective therapies from those that have insufficient data support. Therefore, it is incumbent upon veterinarians engaged in regenerative therapies to be well versed in and contribute to the emerging science of the field in order to successfully select the specific therapeutic protocols, processes, equipment, and vendors most likely to result in clinical benefit for their patients.

## Abstract

Regenerative medicine is defined as the process of replacing or regenerating cells, tissues, or organs to restore or establish normal function. The use of regenerative medicine in equine practice to treat injured musculoskeletal tissues with limited capacity for intrinsic healing is growing. This article provides the practitioner with a brief and basic overview of the regenerative products currently used in equine practice.

Regenerative Medicine
=====================

Regenerative Medicine at Sirius Veterinary Orthopedic Center in Omaha
---------------------------------------------------------------------

Our orthopedic vet animal hospital is now open in your community, and we're proud to offer premier [veterinary services](https://www.siriusvet.com/services) that can help animals of various breeds, sizes, and ages achieve the maximal quality of life. Sirius Veterinary Orthopedic Center is committed to utilizing some of the most advanced surgical and therapeutic techniques, such as regenerative medicine, that can address common animal musculoskeletal problems.

![Image 3: Lady playing with her dog in a park.](https://cdcssl.ibsrv.net/ibimg/smb/960x640_80/webmgr/02/5/c/lady-with-dog-resized.jpg.webp?06297f3c616c1604952e0f686c1cb83a)

For more about regenerative medicine and why you may want to consider this restorative approach for your animal, read on.

**Our Orthopedic Veterinary Team Explains Regenerative Veterinary Medicine**
----------------------------------------------------------------------------

Sometimes, due to advancing age, injury, illness, genetics, or environmental factors, an animal may develop some form of joint problem. Depending on the kind and severity, this joint problem can become quite painful or even debilitating for your pet. Signs of a potential joint problem include limping, lameness, decreased tolerance to or interest in activity, difficulty getting off the floor, and yelping/crying out in pain when attempting to move or bear weight on the affected limb.

Regenerative medicine is a branch of veterinary care that utilizes healthy tissues from your pet's own body to facilitate healing in an affected joint. At Sirius, our orthopedic vet staff offers two main types of regenerative medicine techniques, including:

*   **Platelet Rich Plasma (PRP)** - This therapy uses plasma, a component of normal blood fluid, that has become highly concentrated with specialized blood cells called platelets. A sample of the animal's own blood is taken and processed through a centrifugal and distillation process to produce the platelet rich plasma while also removing red and white blood cells. The remaining extract, otherwise known as PRP, can be re-injected into the animal's body to stimulate healing. This accelerated healing response is due to the platelets' innate ability to regenerate and repair cells while decreasing inflammation.

*   **Stem Cell Therapy** - The "tabula rasa" of the body, stem cells are "undifferentiated," which means they can grow into virtually any tissue in the body. This technique utilizes stem cells extracted from the animal's bone marrow or body fat which are then reintroduced to injured tissues or damaged joints. These stem cells go on to regenerate ligaments/tendons and other intraarticular (inside the joint) structures.

**The Benefits of Regenerative Medicine**
-----------------------------------------

Both PRP and stem cell therapy have a pronounced ability to restore normal joint motion, reduce pain, and improve quality of life for our animal patients. Because the substances used to promote healing come from your animal's own body, the risk of infection and rejection is low, and outcomes are generally positive. Our orthopedic veterinary team in Omaha has used regenerative medicine to help animals suffering from a range of disorders, including:

    *   Osteoarthritis
    *   Tendonitis
    *   Degenerative joint disease
    *   Hip dysplasia
    *   Achilles’ tendon strains and tears 
    *   Elbow dysplasia

**What to Expect with Regenerative Medicine from Our Orthopedic Veterinarian**
------------------------------------------------------------------------------

If you've been referred to us by your primary veterinarian, know that your pet will be in good hands. We work with you to determine the best course of treatment depending on your animal's needs, and do everything in our power to make the experience as stress-free as possible for both you and your animal companion.

If our team decides that regenerative medicine may be an appropriate therapy for your pet, expect our staff to thoroughly review with you all the necessary steps, including how to prepare your animal before and take care of your animal after the surgical procedure. We will explain what signs and symptoms to look out for during your pet's recovery period, and when you should start seeing results. We will also maintain constant communication with your primary veterinarian to ensure continued care for your pet.

Veterinary [regenerative medicine](/en-us/explanations/medicine/biomedicine/regenerative-medicine/) focuses on using stem cells, [tissue engineering](/en-us/explanations/medicine/dentistry/tissue-engineering/), and biologically active molecules to repair or replace damaged tissues and organs in animals. This rapidly evolving field aims to enhance healing processes and improve outcomes for various conditions in pets and livestock, such as [arthritis](/en-us/explanations/medicine/orthopedics-musculoskeletal/arthritis/), tendon injuries, and organ dysfunction. By understanding and applying these advanced biological therapies, veterinarians help optimize animal health, thus providing innovative solutions beyond traditional treatments.

## Veterinary Regenerative Medicine Definition

**Veterinary [Regenerative Medicine](/en-us/explanations/medicine/biomedicine/regenerative-medicine/)** is a rapidly growing field within veterinary care that focuses on repairing or replacing damaged tissues and organs in animals. By utilizing the body's own healing mechanisms and leveraging advanced technologies, this branch of medicine aims to improve the health and [quality of life](/en-us/explanations/medicine/palliative-geriatric-care/quality-of-life/) for animals.

### What is Veterinary Regenerative Medicine?

Regenerative medicine in veterinary practice revolves around the concept of utilizing a variety of therapies to aid in the restoration of function in damaged tissues and organs of animals. This innovative approach is often used in conjunction with traditional treatments to enhance healing and recovery.

Key components of veterinary regenerative medicine include:

These methods are employed to treat a wide range of conditions such as [osteoarthritis](/en-us/explanations/medicine/chronic-diseases/osteoarthritis/), tendon injuries, and various [chronic diseases](/en-us/explanations/medicine/chronic-diseases/), offering a novel approach to veterinary care.

An example of veterinary regenerative medicine in practice is the use of [stem cell therapy](/en-us/explanations/medicine/diagnosis-therapy/stem-cell-therapy/) to treat a dog suffering from hip [dysplasia](/en-us/explanations/medicine/pathology-histology/dysplasia/). By injecting stem cells directly into the affected joint, veterinaries aim to reduce inflammation and regenerate healthy [cartilage](/en-us/explanations/medicine/anatomy/cartilage/), enhancing the animal's mobility and comfort.

**Stem Cell Therapy**: A form of treatment where stem cells are used to promote repair of damaged tissue or organs.

### History and Evolution of Veterinary Regenerative Medicine

The history and evolution of veterinary regenerative medicine are rooted in the advancements in human medical research. Initially, regenerative concepts were primarily focused on human applications. However, over the years, these concepts crossed over to veterinary science, opening new possibilities in animal healthcare.

Milestones in the development of veterinary regenerative medicine include:

In more recent advancements, the integration of 3D bioprinting in veterinary regenerative medicine is being explored. This technology involves printing living cells to fabricate tissue-like structures. Although still experimental, this could revolutionize the field by allowing custom, on-demand treatment options for specialized cases in veterinary care.

Although regenerative medicine holds great promise, not all veterinary conditions can be treated with these methods. A comprehensive [diagnosis](/en-us/explanations/medicine/dermatology/diagnosis/) and consultation with veterinary specialists are essential in determining the appropriateness of such treatments.

## Principles of Regenerative Medicine in Veterinary Science

Regenerative medicine in veterinary science is revolutionizing animal healthcare by harnessing the body’s natural ability to heal itself. This discipline intertwines with several scientific concepts and biological mechanisms.

### Core Concepts and Theories

At the core of **veterinary regenerative medicine** are concepts such as [tissue regeneration](/en-us/explanations/medicine/anatomy/tissue-regeneration/) and [cellular therapy](/en-us/explanations/medicine/diagnosis-therapy/cellular-therapy/), which collectively aim to restore the functionality of damaged tissues in animals. These theories are built around the idea that the body can repair itself given the right stimuli and tools.

Key concepts include:

**Biological Scaffolds:** Structures made of materials that support cells to grow and form new tissues.

An example of these concepts in action is using a biological scaffold in a canine patient with a severe tissue deficit. The scaffold, populated with the dog's own cells, is implanted to support [tissue regeneration](/en-us/explanations/medicine/anatomy/tissue-regeneration/), facilitating natural healing.

A deeper understanding of regenerative medicine theories reveals insights into how [cellular](/en-us/explanations/medicine/biomedicine/cellular/) therapies are crafted. Scientists meticulously select cell types based on their regenerative potential. A popular choice includes [mesenchymal stem cells](/en-us/explanations/medicine/biomedicine/mesenchymal-stem-cells/), known for their ability to modulate immune responses and stimulate tissue repair.

Moreover, researchers are exploring the synergistic potential of combining different types of therapies, such as integrating stem cells with advanced biomaterials to enhance the regenerative process.

### Biological Principles and Mechanisms

The biological principles underlying regenerative medicine are centered around the natural processes of healing and repair found in animals. These principles harness the body's endogenous capacities to facilitate regeneration.

Mechanisms employed include:

Understanding these mechanisms is crucial as they provide a foundation for developing new therapies that could significantly enhance the recovery processes in injured animals.

An illustrative case is that of a horse with a tendon injury. Through cellular proliferation and [signaling pathways](/en-us/explanations/medicine/anatomy/signaling-pathways/), regenerative treatments can stimulate the growth of new, healthy tendon tissue, substantially improving recovery time.

## Veterinary Regenerative Medicine Techniques

Veterinary regenerative medicine techniques represent a transformative advance in animal health care, where biological methods are employed to repair and regenerate damaged tissues and organs. This modern approach focuses on leveraging an animal’s intrinsic healing abilities alongside cutting-edge technologies.

### Common Practices and Approaches

The field of veterinary regenerative medicine includes several widely adopted practices and approaches, each with its own unique applications and benefits. These practices aim to integrate traditional veterinary procedures with innovative treatment methods to enhance recovery outcomes for various conditions.

Key practices include:

Treatments such as these are utilized for a variety of ailments including joint injuries, [ligament](/en-us/explanations/medicine/anatomy/ligament/) tears, and [chronic pain](/en-us/explanations/medicine/chronic-diseases/chronic-pain/), offering significant potential to improve animal welfare.

For instance, a cat with [osteoarthritis](/en-us/explanations/medicine/chronic-diseases/osteoarthritis/) might receive PRP therapy to diminish joint inflammation and pain, enhancing its mobility and [quality of life](/en-us/explanations/medicine/palliative-geriatric-care/quality-of-life/).

Diving deeper, stem cell therapy has evolved significantly, where autoproduction of stem cells derived from the animal itself is favored to circumvent immune rejection. Recent studies focus on optimizing these cells’ environmental conditions to improve their differentiation potential and thus enhance treatment efficacy.

Additionally, multi-pronged approaches combining stem cells with [growth factors](/en-us/explanations/medicine/endocrinology/growth-factors/) hold promise for more comprehensive healing solutions.

In practice, leveraging a combination of therapies can often result in improved outcomes, as these approaches can target multiple aspects of tissue repair simultaneously.

### Innovations and Emerging Techniques

The veterinary regenerative medicine landscape is continuously evolving, with ongoing research unveiling innovative and emerging techniques that promise to further revolutionize animal healthcare. These innovations are geared towards enhancing the precision and effectiveness of existing treatments.

Some of the cutting-edge innovations include:

An innovative use of 3D bioprinting could involve printing a bio-compatible scaffold for a dog with a substantial skin injury, where new cells can grow and restore the lost tissue.

Emerging techniques such as exosome therapy offer a glimpse into the future of non-invasive treatments. Exosomes, tiny particles expelled by cells, have been shown to carry potent proteins and RNA molecules that can effectively influence and coordinate the regeneration processes.

With the potential to reduce inflammation, modulate immune responses, and directly promote tissue repair, exosomes are a frontier that is actively being explored.

While these emerging innovations hold great promise, they are still subject to rigorous research to ensure safety and efficacy before becoming mainstream treatments.

## Stem Cell Therapy in Veterinary Medicine

**Stem cell therapy** is a groundbreaking treatment within [veterinary medicine](/en-us/explanations/medicine/veterinary-medicine/), providing new avenues for healing and recovery in animals. By capitalizing on the unique properties of stem cells, this therapy presents significant advantages for the veterinary field.

### Applications and Benefits

Stem cell therapy is applied to various veterinary cases, bringing several benefits and improving treatment outcomes for animals. It is primarily used to address conditions where traditional methods might fall short.

Notable applications include:

The benefits of stem cell therapy in [veterinary medicine](/en-us/explanations/medicine/veterinary-medicine/) include:

An example includes treating a racehorse with tendon damage using stem cell therapy. This approach not only accelerates the [healing process](/en-us/explanations/medicine/anatomy/healing-process/) but also strengthens the tendons, allowing the horse to return to full activity sooner than with conventional treatment alone.

Stem cell therapy often pairs well with other regenerative techniques, providing a holistic approach to veterinary treatment that can maximize healing potential.

### Current Research and Developments

Current research in the field of stem cell therapy for veterinary medicine is rapidly advancing, with numerous developments aiming to enhance the efficacy and safety of these treatments. Scientists are continuously exploring the diverse capabilities of stem cells and how they can be harnessed to treat more complex veterinary conditions.

Key areas of research include:

A fascinating area of study is the use of induced pluripotent stem (iPS) cells in veterinary medicine. iPS cells are adult cells reprogrammed to an embryonic stem cell-like state, allowing them to differentiate into any cell type required for regeneration. This technology has the potential to revolutionize the field, offering patient-specific treatments without the ethical concerns associated with [embryonic stem cells](/en-us/explanations/medicine/biomedicine/embryonic-stem-cells/).

Researchers are also focused on integrating stem cell therapies with other advanced techniques like 3D bioprinting, to create comprehensive [treatment plans](/en-us/explanations/medicine/diagnosis-therapy/treatment-plans/) that not only aid in regeneration but also provide structure and support to damaged tissues.

## Veterinary Regenerative Medicine Examples

An increasing number of real-world examples highlight the impact of **veterinary regenerative medicine** across various cases and breeds. These examples demonstrate how regenerative therapies are transforming the way animals are treated, offering hope for chronic and severe conditions.

### Case Studies and Real-World Scenarios

Exploring case studies and real-world scenarios provides valuable insights into how veterinary regenerative medicine is applied in practice. These instances showcase the potential of such treatments in both common and exceptional conditions faced by animals.

Some noteworthy case studies include:

For instance, a veterinary clinic conducted a successful stem cell treatment on a Labrador retriever with severe hip dysplasia. The treatment reduced inflammation, decreased pain, and improved joint function, allowing the dog to return to normal activities.

Real-world scenarios reveal that regenerative medicine can significantly reduce the necessity for invasive surgeries, presenting a less risky and often more affordable alternative for pet owners.

### Success Stories and Treatments

Success stories in veterinary regenerative medicine underscore the therapy's potential, illustrating numerous cases where animals have shown remarkable recovery and resilience following treatment. These stories speak to both the efficacy and promise of regenerative therapies.

Highlighting significant treatments:

A deep exploration reveals the integration of regenerative medicine into veterinary [orthopedics](/en-us/explanations/medicine/history-of-medicine/orthopedics/), with therapies now serving as primary treatments for debilitating [joint disorders](/en-us/explanations/medicine/anatomy/joint-disorders/). Interestingly, research into combining stem cells with cutting-edge gene therapy aims to enhance recovery further, offering solutions that not only repair but may also completely restore original [tissue function](/en-us/explanations/medicine/anatomy/tissue-function/).

Additionally, collaborative efforts are in place to standardize these treatments, ensuring they deliver consistent results and are accessible across diverse veterinary practices globally.

enriched_points.expanded_content: Regenerative therapies involve biological treatments that restore or replace damaged cells, tissues, or organs, harnessing the body's inherent healing capabilities to promote recovery.
These therapies address conditions lacking effective treatments, offering veterinarians innovative solutions to enhance animal health, manage chronic diseases, and improve recovery outcomes.
Regenerative therapies utilize the body's own stem cells, growth factors, and biologically active molecules to stimulate repair processes, enhancing tissue regeneration and healing.
This presentation will explore various regenerative therapy techniques, their applications in veterinary practice, regulatory considerations, and the future potential for animal health improvements.
The integration of regenerative therapies in veterinary medicine promises to revolutionize treatment approaches, offering new hope for animals with previously untreatable conditions.
